title: Talking in Code
description: Work with text data using string variables - learn to store and manipulate words and messages.

preDialogues.name: Maru
preDialogues.content: Variables aren't just for numbers! You can also store text, which we call 'strings' in programming.

preDialogues.name: Qubit
preDialogues.content: Strings? Like... a string of letters?

preDialogues.name: Maru
preDialogues.content: Exactly! Strings are text wrapped in quotes. Try creating: crop_type = "wheat"

preDialogues.name: Qubit
preDialogues.content: So the quotes tell the computer this is text, not code?

preDialogues.name: Maru
preDialogues.content: Precisely! Without quotes, wheat would be a variable name. With quotes, "wheat" is text data.

postDialogues.name: Maru
postDialogues.content: Perfect! You've created a string variable. Now you can use crop_type wherever you need that text.

preDialogues.name: Maru
preDialogues.content: You can combine strings using the + operator. Try: message = "Growing " + crop_type

preDialogues.name: Qubit
preDialogues.content: So if crop_type is "wheat", message becomes "Growing wheat"?

preDialogues.name: Maru
preDialogues.content: Exactly! This is called concatenation—joining strings together.

postDialogues.name: Maru
postDialogues.content: Great! String concatenation lets you build dynamic messages.

preDialogues.name: Maru
preDialogues.content: There's an even better way to put variables in strings: f-strings! Try: message = f"Planting {crop_type} now"

preDialogues.name: Qubit
preDialogues.content: The f before the quote and curly braces around the variable?

preDialogues.name: Maru
preDialogues.content: Yes! f-strings automatically insert variable values where you put {variable_name}. It's cleaner than using +.

objectives.description: Create an f-string with a variable

postDialogues.name: Qubit
postDialogues.content: F-strings are so much more readable!

postDialogues.name: Maru
postDialogues.content: They're one of Python's most loved features!

preDialogues.name: Maru
preDialogues.content: Now the practical part! Remember plant() can take a crop type? Try: plant(crop_type) where crop_type is your string variable.

preDialogues.name: Qubit
preDialogues.content: Oh! So I can change crop_type once, and all my plant() calls use the new value?

preDialogues.name: Maru
preDialogues.content: Exactly! That's the flexibility of variables. Change one line, and the whole program adapts.

objectives.description: Use a string variable with plant: plant(crop_type)
Execute your code with string variables

postDialogues.name: Qubit
postDialogues.content: This makes my code so much more flexible! I can easily switch crops!

postDialogues.name: Maru
postDialogues.content: Precisely! Variables make your code adaptable. Next, we'll learn about storing position data!
